author: @1943Grandpa
text: 8 years ago I picked up an almost unused Miami Sun Trike for $200. I replace the 3 speed internal hub with a 5 sped, and pedaled it for 6 years.   I am now 78 years old.  Two years ago , I put an Ebikling front wheel kit on it.  1200 watt  motor, 52 volt 20 amp hour battery.  I have put 1,500 miles on that motor, and it still gets 40 miles per charge, and I have no idea how fast it will go, because I will not ride it faster than 20 mph.  I run dogs with the trike, and one young dog walks fast at 12 mph.  He runs easily at 16 mph for 5 miles.   I love that trike.  they will tip over easily.  I like my 24 " wheels.  I put hard rubber tires on it so I never get a flat.  A little hard on the old body, but we need to stay tough as long as we can.  I put $1,000 into my trike.  If I have  get a new one I will likely get the exact same thing.  Oh!  I am substantially paralyzed on the left side, and can barely walk, but I discovered that I can peddle  8-9 years ago.

replies.author: @georgiana6598

author: @nickwinn7812
text: Less than £300 quid if you are very lucky to find a super cheap motor and super cheap controller/throttle. and if batteries are free. A 1000W bike is not legal on the british roads at any speed. The speed you refer to is for throttle only on a bike which is normally pedelec. The maximum legal  motor power (that is rated power, not peak) is 250W. Your motor looks like it's rated 1000W so could probably handle peak power of double that. You have an electric moped, which must conform to the construction and use regs, be insured, you need a licence, helmet etc. I would prefer to live in your world but unfortunately we have to live in the regulatory framework we have or campaign to change it. Please - I hope nobody gets nicked for following your advice.

author: @TechKnowCSS
text: That MY1020 500 watt motor is 24 volts at about 2500 rpm at about 15 MPH (24KPH) , well it can handle 36 volts which makes it 1000 watts at 3750 rpm which will bring you to the maximum legal 20 MPH (32KPH). You only made one ERROR, you forgot the freewheel ($10) for the motor gear on the axle. Without it you will be peddling while dragging the motor which makes it harder to pedal and the motor is now a generator.  A 36 volt motor controller is about the same price but if the one you have has 50 volt capacitors you can use that one if not change the capacitors to 50 volt same capacity or just buy another controller. CHEERS.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest a flywheel doesn't sound right - I would think you could just break the live on the motor with a switch - no circuit - no work. It seems to me a flywheel adds a workload but stores the energy for later

replies.author: @TechKnowCSS
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering No matter what you do you will be fighting the permanent magnets all the time you pedal, plus the drag of the chain and sprockets. Not ideal if you are old like we are, but would provide a great workout for the young....lol. CHEERS.

replies.author: @rhysstatham7407
replies.text: Rik rik i agree with your comments they make perfect sense I would also say that all 250watt motors that are road legal are peaking there power way above 250watts more like 550watts and higher otherwise you wouldn’t be able to pull heavy loads or climb steeper hills. I currently own 36volt electric wheel kit That’s rated at 350watts but the controller is rated at 7amps continuous making the motor output 252watts but a Peake of 15 amps making it 540watts.i agree it shouldn’t be about Watts but about speed that you travel.

replies.author: @danielking2944
